y9kfj_v3.title: Forensic Outpatient Cognitive Understanding Study (FOCUS): A study protocol
y9kfj_v3.description:
Background: There is growing evidence that cognitive dysfunction plays a role in the emergence and persistence of antisocial behavior, and it is increasingly implicated as relevant for risk assessment and treatment within the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) framework. However, this evidence comes predominantly from prison and inpatient populations. Whether similar patterns hold in forensic outpatient settings remains largely unknown. Moreover, existing studies often rely on normative groups that are not matched for age, sex, and education, and tend to focus on single cognitive domains rather than administering comprehensive test batteries.
Methods: This protocol paper describes the design and methods of the FOCUS study, which aims to map cognitive functioning in forensic outpatients and examine its associations with aggression and treatment outcomes. The study compares forensic outpatients (n = 100) to a normative group (n = 100, matched on sex, age, and educational level). All participants complete a comprehensive computerized neuropsychological test battery covering seven cognitive domains: intelligence, attention, language, executive functioning, social cognition, memory, and decision making. Aggression is assessed using both a self-report questionnaire (Reactive Proactive Questionnaire; RPQ) and a behavioral task (Competitive Reaction Time Task; CRTT). We have four research aims: (1) to compare the cognitive profile of forensic outpatients to a matched normative group; (2) to examine the correspondence between self-reported and task-based aggression; (3) to examine associations between neuropsychological test scores and aggression; and (4) to examine associations between neuropsychological test scores and treatment outcomes, including recidivism risk, treatment non-attendance, dropout, and actual recidivism. 
Discussion: The FOCUS study is the first to administer a comprehensive neuropsychological test battery in a forensic outpatient sample with a matched normative comparison group. Findings are expected to advance understanding of cognitive functioning within the RNR framework and to inform more individualized forensic treatment planning.

7ehdm_v2.title: Impact of a Pre-Test Measurement of Alcohol Craving: Lessons From a Self-Replication Study
7ehdm_v2.description:
A prior study during COVID-19 lockdown (Heck et al., 2024) examined whether a pre-test alcohol craving measurement influenced posttest assessment and tested its relationship with social desirability (SD) and demand effects (the consequences of participants trying to uncover the study’s aim). Results suggested that the first craving measurement increased its post-experimental levels in heavy drinkers, with no associations found for SD or demand effects. Engagement with the alcohol-related video was linked to craving. This study aimed to replicate these findings three years later outside the lockdown context.
A hundred and forty-two psychology students were randomly allocated to either a “pre-post” or a “post-only” craving assessment group. They watched an alcohol-related video. SD, demand effects, engagement, and alcohol consumption levels were also assessed. 
Several findings supported the robustness of effects observed in the initial study, while others diverged, offering new hypotheses. Heavier drinkers reported significantly higher craving levels following video exposure and craving correlated moderately with video engagement. No significant associations were found between craving and SD or demand characteristics. However, unlike the initial study, repeated measurements showed no significant effects or interactions with alcohol consumption.
Reanalysis of the original data highlighted a gender-based interaction, where the effect of the interaction between repeated craving measurements and alcohol consumption was significant in men but not in women. These results suggest partial replication of prior findings and underscore the need to further explore contextual and demographic influences on alcohol craving. Moreover, this study confirmed a significant role of video engagement in craving.


Contact e-mail: equertemont@uliege.be or m.heck@uliege.be

p6nmv_v1.title: Autistic adults integrate the timing of beat gestures  and spoken prosody
p6nmv_v1.description: In face-to-face communication, speakers provide several visual cues that facilitate speech comprehension for the listener. For example, they commonly make up-and-down flicks of the hand, called “beat gestures”, to emphasize a specific part of their speech (e.g., syllables, biasing the perceived lexical stress in words like CONtent vs. conTENT). These cues contribute to audiovisual speech integration, where listeners combine auditory speech with visual information from the speaker. Early research suggests that autistic children may show attenuated audiovisual speech integration effects (i.e., a reduced McGurk effect). However, more recent findings with adults show similar integration of speech and lip movements or meaningful gestures as non-autistic individuals. The present online large-scale study tested whether the timing of beat gestures biases audiovisual stress perception in autistic (N=101) and non-autistic (N=65) adults. Beat gestures affected speech perception to a similar extent in autistic and non-autistic adults. No relationship was observed between audiovisual integration and autistic traits (Autism Quotient-28 scores) or the use and understanding of visual communicative signals and gestures in daily life (Action and Feelings Questionnaire scores). Overall, these results converge with recent findings challenging the notion that audiovisual integration is altered in autism, at least in adulthood.

2hsbm_v1.title: Too anxious to learn:  Induced state anxiety prevents the formation of nocebo hyperalgesia
2hsbm_v1.description: Nocebo hyperalgesia is characterized by a pain increase following an inactive intervention. Existing research on the mechanisms of nocebo effects has predominantly centered around the role of cognitive processes, leaving emotional processes relatively understudied. A recently proposed theoretical model on the role of negative emotions in the development of placebo effects in pain suggests that heightened anxiety may enhance nocebo hyperalgesia. The objective of the present study is to investigate the impact of pretreatment and posttreatment anxiety on the magnitude of nocebo hyperalgesia induced through classical conditioning. Healthy volunteers (N=120) were randomized to one of three groups: 1) classical conditioning of nocebo hyperalgesia preceded by anxiety induction (ANC); 2) classical conditioning of nocebo hyperalgesia (NC); and 3) no-manipulation control (CTRL). Anxiety was induced by the anticipation of an electric shock. During the conditioning, a placebo was paired with high-intensity heat pain stimuli, while moderate-intensity heat pain stimuli were provided without a placebo. Pretreatment anxiety disrupted the formation of nocebo hyperalgesia: although nocebo hyperalgesia was found in the NC group, no effect was observed in the ANC group. Nocebo conditioning changed the pain-related expectancies in the NC group: participants expected more pain than the CTRL group. Nocebo conditioning did not cause the development of posttreatment anxiety. Neither pain-related expectancies nor posttreatment anxiety mediated nocebo hyperalgesia. Because pretreatment anxiety influenced the formation of nocebo hyperalgesia, healthcare workers may consider patients’ anxiety level when treating pain as it may predict treatment responses.

rkb7a_v1.title: The impact of cash out availability on online betting behavior
rkb7a_v1.description: The rise of online sports betting, facilitated by technological innovations such as the cash-out feature, has transformed the gambling landscape. While cash-outs (a facility provided by betting operators which allows gamblers to close a bet early before the final outcome of a sports event is known) have been suggested to intensify problematic gambling behaviors, empirical studies to date have been correlational, leaving a gap in determining causal evidence. The present Registered Report investigated the direct impact of the perceived availability of the cash-out feature on various facets of gambling behavior in an online experiment. Hungarian residents with an online gambling history (N=595) were informed that they would have the chance to gamble with real money on sports events. Following random allocation, half of the participants were told that they would have the option to cash out their bets before the outcome of the sports event is known, while the other half did not have this option. Overall, the findings provided evidence that cash-out availability did not influence bet placement, wager size, or bet risk among our sample. However, the low variance in the outcomes may have limited the ability to detect subtle differences. Consequently, the null results should not be taken as conclusive evidence of no behavioral effect.

7u2rm_v2.title: The Nature of Mind-Wandering to Secrets.
7u2rm_v2.description: Secrets are on people’s minds more than they are held back in conversation. Research suggests that such mind-wandering to secrets can be problematic for well-being, yet the mechanisms behind this link are still unclear. To better understand what may underlie the harm of mind-wandering to secrets, we conducted two studies. First, in a 14-day daily diary study (Study 1, N = 240) we investigated what people think about when mind-wandering to their secrets, and how these thoughts come to mind in daily life—spontaneously or deliberately. Worries and concerns about secrets intruded on the mind spontaneously, whereas when people intentionally mind-wandered to a secret, they fantasized and daydreamt about it (Study 1). Second, in a 7-day experience-sampling study (Study 2, N = 207) we probed the directionality of relationships between the two types of mind-wandering and affective experiences about secrets. Across studies, when secrets came to mind spontaneously, but not deliberately, people reported feeling greater negative emotion both in the moment (Study 1 and 2) and approximately 2 hours later (Study 2). This work offers the first investigation into spontaneous and deliberate mind-wandering to the same target content in daily life, capturing the temporal dynamics between mind-wandering and emotional well-being. Our findings point to spontaneous mind-wandering to secrets as a primary player in the well-being costs of secrecy. By yielding insights into the way secrets weigh on people’s minds, this research suggests possible ways to manage the well-being costs of secrets in everyday life.

4s9jk_v1.title: Promoting well-doing: A Positive Psychology intervention for regulating performance momentum and thriving among high achievers
4s9jk_v1.description: The present study examined a novel journalling intervention designed to support subjective wellbeing and perceived work performance among high achievers. Sixty-seven university students reporting a grade average of B+ or higher completed a one-week, within-subjects pre–post study involving a daily ‘well done’ journalling task focused on small actions done well. Subjective wellbeing and perceived work performance were measured pre- and post- intervention; with journalling frequency and qualitative feedback collected afterwards. Findings indicated no detectable change in wellbeing from an elevated baseline, however, a significant improvement in perceived work performance was observed. Post-hoc analyses revealed a dose–response pattern, with stronger performance improvements among participants with frequent task engagement. Qualitative feedback was generally favourable, suggesting perceived benefits in positive affect, self-esteem, and motivation. Overall, results show preliminary support for ‘well done’ journalling to enhance perceived performance in high-achieving students.
